My own grandson is always complaining about that stuff he sees in his feeds – you know how teenagers can be, with their drama and fake news. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I'm quite certain that you can't really do much about it, since I don't use any social media myself. But from what my kids tell me when they come over, there's just too much misinformation floating around online these days. It makes me a bit anxious to think about all those people believing false things.

They say some folks fall for conspiracy theories on Facebook or Twitter. My own grandson is always complaining about that stuff he sees in his feeds – you know how teenagers can be, with their drama and fake news. He tells me if someone posts something controversial online, they're likely lying anyway.

I used to volunteer at the library teaching seniors computer skills back when I was still working there years ago. We talked a lot about how to avoid being scammed or tricked by bad information on websites like what we have now – social media platforms.

But these days? It all just seems so overwhelming and confusing for me personally, since I don't actually browse those sites myself anymore.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The user persona is a retired woman with no formal education but moderate to higher levels in agreeableness and neuroticism. She doesn't use social media herself, which limits her ability to offer concrete advice on the topic. Her anxiety about fake content likely stems from observing family members' experiences or general societal concerns she overhears.
